originalTitle: The market for psychedelic drugs to treat mental health conditions gets a big buy signal
summary: Eli Lilly's acquisition of psychedelic drug maker AtaiBeckley extends Big Pharma's embrace of a stigmatized class of medications for treating mental health.
content:
Thursday's announcement that Eli Lilly will acquire AtaiBeckley , a clinical-stage maker of psychedelic drugs, for up to $3.8 billion indicates Big Pharma's increasing embrace of a long-stigmatized class of medications as alternatives for treating an array of mental health conditions. The news comes on the heels of positive clinical trial results of several different psychedelics, as well as President Trump's executive order in April fast-tracking reviews of such drugs by the Food and Drug Administration.

AtaiBeckley's lead asset, BPL-003, is a synthetic form of 5-MeO-DMT — derived from the venom of Sonoran Desert toads —administered as an intranasal spray for treatment-resistant depression, a severe form of the illness that ​does not improve after standard treatments, which affects around four million Americans. A year ago, Atai reported results of its Phase 2b study of BPL-003, which demonstrated rapid and durable reductions in TRD symptoms. AtaiBeckley is developing several other psychedelics, including one related to MDMA, colloquially known as ecstasy.

BPL-003 has been granted Breakthrough Therapy Designation by the FDA, a program that expedites drug development and review, and is currently in Phase 3 trials. "Phase 3 success by early 2029 could open up a $1-2 billion opportunity in TRD," Jefferies analysts wrote in a note. More generally, they wrote, "our KOLs [key opinion leaders] confirm psychedelics could represent the biggest change to psychiatry and are possibly a paradigm shift, with patient demand already seeming insatiable."

5-MeO-DMT joins several other psychedelic substances — LSD, psilocybin (aka magic mushrooms), MDMA, ayahuasca and ibogaine — that biopharma disrupters have formulated and are successfully testing to treat depression, post traumatic stress disorder, anxiety, eating disorders and alcohol and drug addiction.

Psychedelic drugs appear to temporarily loosen the brain's rigid patterns of communication, allowing regions that don't usually "talk" much to connect in new ways. In that more flexible state, especially when paired with therapy, people may be able to revisit trauma, depression or addiction from a different perspective and form healthier mental pathways. Some experts refer to this process as "rewiring" the brain.

"There's enough data generated so far that suggests that this class of drugs can deliver something more meaningful than what the orals have been able to do so far," said Ami Fadia, a biotech analyst at Needham, referring to SSRIs and SNRIs, medications widely prescribed for more than three decades to treat a broad range of psychiatric illnesses. "A few years ago, some investors would not want to touch [psychedelics], but now I rarely hear that," she said.

Indeed, it's been a long, strange trip for psychedelics. Psychiatric scientists began studying them in the 1960s, producing promising results. But they also became popular recreational drugs among the counterculture (next month marks the 57th anniversary of Woodstock), leading Congress to pass the Controlled Substances Act in 1970. Psychedelics were designated as federally outlawed Schedule I drugs deemed to have no medical benefit. Research projects came to a halt.

After languishing in the R&D doldrums for years, the drugs found their way back into researchers' labs and clinics beginning in the late 1990s and early 2000s, a reemergence documented by nonfiction author Michael Pollan in his 2018 best-seller, "How to Change Your Mind," in which he wrote that "after several decades of suppression and neglect, psychedelics are having a renaissance."

Recent clinical trial boost for psychedelics

The clinical trial path of psychedelics has featured notable stumbles in recent years, most notably in August 2024 when the FDA denied approval of an MDMA-assisted therapy for PTSD developed by Lykos Therapeutics. This marked the first time the agency had considered a Schedule I drug for medical use, so the denial was not only a blow to Lykos but to the wider movement to bring psychedelic drugs into mainstream. "It's a huge blow to the field," said Dr. Boris Heifets, an anesthesiologist at Stanford University whose lab studies psychedelics, at the time.

Despite that setback, clinical trials for psychedelics have continued apace with positive outcomes. For example, Definium Therapeutics released positive results from its Phase 3 clinical trials of the company's LSD-based drug, DT120, showing that it significantly helped patients with major depressive disorder. The biopharmaceutical startup's shares skyrocketed by 50%.

The data's "effect size is the largest I've ever seen," said Jefferies analyst Andrew Tsai. "What's special about Definium is that that same drug, LSD, has already shown very profound anxiety data as well, and we're going to get two more Phase 3 studies [for general anxiety disorder] this month and in September," Tsai said.

If those results prove to be similarly robust, the company can file what's called a New Drug Application with the FDA within the next six to nine months, said Definium CEO Robert Barrow. "Even under an expedited review," he said, referencing the president's executive order, "the review lasts about eight months."

Importantly, because LSD is still a banned Schedule I drug, Definium also will need the Drug Enforcement Administration to reclassify it. Barrow expects that DT120, "presumably with all its safety controls in place, to be scheduled certainly below Schedule I," he said. Individual states will have to reclassify LSD in this case as well.

Another hurdle in bringing psychedelics to market is having them covered by insurance, which requires coding for each drug, a process overseen by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services. "Coverage is going to be necessary for patients to get treatment," Fadia said. "Each company will need to negotiate with payers about the cost of the drug."

chineseTitle: 治疗心理健康问题的致幻药物市场收到强烈买入信号
chineseSummary: 礼来公司 (Eli Lilly) 对迷幻药物制造商 AtaiBeckley 的收购扩大了大型制药公司对治疗心理健康的一类被污名化的药物的接受程度。
chineseContent:
周四，礼来公司宣布将以高达 38 亿美元的价格收购临床阶段迷幻药物制造商 AtaiBeckley ，这表明大型制药公司越来越多地接受长期被污名化的一类药物作为治疗一系列精神健康疾病的替代药物。这一消息是在几种不同迷幻药的积极临床试验结果以及特朗普总统四月份发布的行政命令之后发布的，美国食品和药物管理局对此类药物进行快速审查。

AtaiBeckley 的主要资产 BPL-003 是 5-MeO-DMT 的合成形式（源自索诺兰沙漠蟾蜍的毒液），作为鼻内喷雾剂用于治疗难治性抑郁症，这是一种严重的疾病，在标准治疗后不会改善，影响了约 400 万美国人。一年前，Atai 报告了 BPL-003 2b 期研究的结果，该研究表明 TRD 症状可以快速而持久地减少。 AtaiBeckley 正在开发其他几种迷幻药，其中包括一种与 MDMA（俗称摇头丸）相关的迷幻药。

BPL-003 已被 FDA 授予突破性治疗指定，该计划旨在加快药物开发和审查，目前正在进行 3 期试验。 Jefferies 分析师在一份报告中写道：“到 2029 年初，第三阶段的成功可能会为 TRD 带来 1-2​​0 亿美元的机会。”更广泛地说，他们写道，“我们的 KOL（关键意见领袖）确认迷幻药可能代表精神病学的最大变化，并且可能是一种范式转变，患者的需求似乎已经无法满足。”

5-MeO-DMT 与其他几种迷幻物质——LSD、裸盖菇素（又名迷幻蘑菇）、MDMA、死藤水和伊博加因——一起被生物制药颠覆者配制并成功测试用于治疗抑郁症、创伤后应激障碍、焦虑、饮食失调以及酒精和药物成瘾。

迷幻药似乎可以暂时放松大脑僵化的沟通模式，让通常不“说话”的区域以新的方式进行联系。在这种更灵活的状态下，尤其是与治疗相结合时，人们可能能够从不同的角度重新审视创伤、抑郁或成瘾，并形成更健康的心理路径。一些专家将这个过程称为“重新布线”大脑。

Needham 的生物技术分析师 Ami Fadia 表示：“迄今为止产生的足够数据表明，这类药物可以提供比迄今为止口服更有意义的药物。”他指的是 SSRI 和 SNRI，这两种药物在三十多年来被广泛用于治疗多种精神疾病。 “几年前，一些投资者不想接触[迷幻药]，但现在我很少听到这种说法，”她说。

事实上，对于迷幻剂来说，这是一次漫长而奇怪的旅程。精神病学科学家在 20 世纪 60 年代开始研究它们，并取得了有希望的结果。但它们也成为反主流文化中流行的消遣性药物（下个月是伍德斯托克音乐节 57 周年），促使国会于 1970 年通过了《管制物质法案》。迷幻药被指定为联邦政府禁止的一级药物，被认为没有医疗益处。研究项目陷入停滞。

在研发低迷多年后，这些药物从 20 世纪 90 年代末和 2000 年代初开始重新回到研究人员的实验室和诊所，非小说类作家迈克尔·波伦 (Michael Pollan) 在其 2018 年畅销书《如何改变你的想法》中记录了这种药物的重新出现，他在书中写道，“经过几十年的压制和忽视，致幻剂正在复兴。”

最近的迷幻药临床试验取得进展

近年来，致幻剂的临床试验路径遭遇了显着的挫折，最引人注目的是 2024 年 8 月，FDA 拒绝批准 Lykos Therapeutics 开发的一种 MDMA 辅助疗法治疗 PTSD。这标志着该机构首次考虑将第一类药物用于医疗用途，因此这一拒绝不仅对 Lykos 是一个打击，而且对更广泛的将迷幻药物纳入主流的运动也是一个打击。 “这对这个领域是一个巨大的打击，”当时斯坦福大学麻醉师鲍里斯·海菲茨 (Boris Heifets) 博士说，他的实验室研究迷幻药。

尽管遭遇挫折，致幻剂的临床试验仍在快速进行，并取得了积极的成果。例如，Definium Therapeutics 发布了该公司基于 LSD 的药物 DT120 的 3 期临床试验的积极结果，表明该药物对重度抑郁症患者有显着帮助。这家生物制药初创公司的股价飙升了 50%。

杰富瑞 (Jefferies) 分析师安德鲁·蔡 (Andrew Tsai) 表示，该数据的“效应规模是我所见过的最大的”。 “Definium 的特别之处在于，同一种药物 LSD 也已经显示出非常深刻的焦虑数据，我们将在本月和 9 月份再进行两项 [针对一般性焦虑症] 的 3 期研究，”Tsai 说。

Definium 首席执行官罗伯特·巴罗 (Robert Barrow) 表示，如果这些结果证明同样强劲，该公司可以在未来六到九个月内向 FDA 提交所谓的新药申请。 “即使是在快速审查的情况下，”他在提到总统的行政命令时说，“审查也会持续大约八个月。”

重要的是，由于 LSD 仍然是一级禁用药物，Definium 还需要缉毒局对其进行重新分类。巴罗预计 DT120“大概在所有安全控制措施都到位的情况下，肯定会低于附表 I”，他说。在这种情况下，各个州也必须对LSD重新分类。

将迷幻药推向市场的另一个障碍是保险范围内的药物，这需要对每种药物进行编码，这一过程由医疗保险和医疗补助服务中心监督。 “患者获得治疗需要保险，”法迪亚说。 “每家公司都需要与付款人就药物的费用进行谈判。”

whyItMatters: 这条信息属于财经市场信号，适合用于观察利率、汇率、股债商品、金融机构和宏观政策变化。
